diagrams.title: Class C Subnetting Breakdown
diagrams.description: Visual representation of a Class C network (192.168.1.0/24) being subdivided into smaller subnets using different subnet masks, showing the binary and decimal representations of network and host portions.
elements: Binary representation of IP and subnet mask
Network address, broadcast address, and usable host range for each subnet
diagrams.purpose: Explains how subnetting works by borrowing bits from the host portion to create smaller network segments, showing the mathematical relationship between subnet masks and resulting subnet sizes.

diagrams.title: Router Packet Forwarding Process
diagrams.description: Diagram showing how a router uses its routing table and subnet mask to determine the next hop for an incoming packet, demonstrating the ANDing operation between destination IP and subnet mask.
elements: Router routing table entries (destination network, subnet mask, next hop, interface)
Binary AND operation visualization (IP AND Mask = Network Address)
Outgoing interface and next hop router
diagrams.purpose: Illustrates the packet forwarding decision process in routers, showing how subnet masks are used to match destination addresses against routing table entries using longest prefix match.

diagrams.title: Routing Algorithm Types Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of Distance Vector (e.g., RIP) and Link State (e.g., OSPF) routing algorithms, showing their operation, advantages, and differences in how they build routing tables.
elements: Distance Vector box: periodic full table exchange, hop count metric, routing by rumor
Link State box: incremental LSP flooding, Dijkstra SPF calculation, complete network map
diagrams.purpose: Demonstrates the fundamental differences between the two main routing algorithm families, helping students understand when each is appropriate and how they each determine optimal paths.
